fact: June 28 sits exactly one week after the summer solstice, which means the days are still super long but they're secretly getting shorter by tiny amounts each day!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for fireflies to put on their most dazzling light shows - they blink faster when it's warmer!
Down in Antarctica, June 28 falls smack in the middle of winter, where the sun barely peeks above the horizon and penguins huddle together in giant, shuffling masses to stay warm.
Most places north of the equator experience their warmest average temperatures right around this date, even though the longest day already passed a week ago.
June 28 marks day 179 of the year in regular years and day 180 in leap years - making it almost exactly halfway through the calendar!
Cancer zodiac season is in full swing on this date, with the moon having maximum influence during summer's peak.
In many northern forests, this is when baby deer lose their white spots and start looking like mini versions of their parents instead of walking polka-dot rugs.
Mosquitoes reach peak annoyance levels around June 28 in most temperate climates because the combination of heat and recent spring rains creates perfect breeding conditions.
The sun sets at its most northwestern point on the horizon around this date if you live in the Northern Hemisphere, creating those weird long shadows that stretch in unexpected directions.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, June 28 brings the shortest days and longest nights, with sunrise happening super late and sunset arriving embarrassingly early.
Many fruit trees hit their growth spurt peak right around this date, with apples, peaches, and cherries swelling up like tiny green balloons getting ready to ripen.
Ocean temperatures in the Northern Hemisphere are still climbing toward their yearly peak, making late June perfect for sea creatures to have their wildest underwater dance parties.
